groups.context_description: Term names the corruption or wasting of the inner self — the progressive destruction of the mind and inner person through sin, deception, or the passage of time
Term names the corruption of the inner self — the progressive moral decay of the inner person through sin, deceitful desires, and false teaching
Term names the destruction of the person who desecrates what belongs to God — the judgment that mirrors the inner violation committed
Term names the corruption of the inner moral life — acting corruptly before God as the expression of the person's inward turn away from his ways
Term names self-destruction as the consequence of inner moral failure — the person who corrupts themselves bringing ruin upon themselves
Term names the divine act of destruction as the response to inner moral corruption — God's judgment that mirrors the people's own corrupted way
Term names the physical marring of the Servant as the outer expression of the inner-being cost of vicarious suffering — the corruption of appearance as the vehicle of redemptive identification

Term names the pit/grave as a moral and existential reality — the trap that wicked persons set for others falls back on themselves, confronting the inner person with mortality and moral accountability
Term names Gehenna as the eschatological consequence of inner moral failure — the destination that orients the inner life toward radical moral seriousness and self-discipline
Term names Gehenna as the source of inner corruption — the hellish origin of moral destruction that corrupts from within
Term names Hades as the realm that threatens the soul — the place of the dead whose power over the inner person is overcome by Christ, grounding inner security against the fear of death
Term names corruption/decay as the condition the inner person faces in death — and the overcoming of this corruption through resurrection as the ground of inner hope
Term names corruption as the inner condition of those enslaved to sinful desire — the decay of the inner person who sows to the flesh rather than the Spirit
Term names corruption as the cosmic condition from which the inner person and creation longs to be freed — the bondage of perishability overcome in resurrection
Term names the pit/Sheol as the threat to the soul — the place of death from which the inner person cries out to God for preservation and rescue
Deception as the act of inner betrayal — violating trust through deliberate misleading
Slackness/deceit as inner disposition — parallel to H7423A
Deceitfulness as the inner disposition from which false speech and resistance to God originate
Enticement — the luring of the inner person through its own desires
Purity — the absence of deceit as the quality of what the inner person longs for
Deceitfulness as an inner-being character quality
Deception as the expression of inner moral corruption through speech
Distortion of truth as a violation of inner-being integrity — and its renunciation
Dealing falsely as a violation of inner-being integrity — and its refusal as faithfulness
Deceit as the inner orientation of the wicked — deceitful tongue, deceitful heart
Deceit as inner character quality and its absence as integrity
Deceit as an inner quality and its absence as the mark of integrity
Treachery as covert inner-being betrayal
Falsehood as the inner orientation of the wicked — lying tongue and lying heart
Prophetic falsehood — lies spoken in God's name corrupting inner spiritual life
Abhorrence of falsehood — the inner-being posture of the righteous
Deceit as the inner mechanism that corrupts desire, hardens the heart, and takes the person captive
Harm inflicted and inner assurance against it — the fear of harm and God's protection
Innocence as inner character — guileless purity and vulnerability to deception
Evil thoughts and the evil heart — wickedness as the inner origin of all harmful action
The evil one — the personal agent of evil as inner spiritual threat and opponent
Evil person and evil generation — inner moral character defining a person or community
Abhorring evil and fleeing the evil day — the inner moral response of turning away from evil
Term names human malicious scheming — inner plotting directed harmfully against another
Term names moral corruption as an inner quality expressed outwardly in conduct
Term names divine purposing — resolute inner intention of God, explicitly located in heart and mind
Evil deeds brought to judgement — the moral evaluation of the whole life before God
Wickedness as inner moral condition — greed, malice and the spiritual forces of evil
Speaking evil from inner stubbornness — hostile speech against the gospel rooted in unbelief
The wrongdoer as moral category — the inner character that produces harmful conduct
Blasphemy as inner contempt and rebellion — the hardened heart that curses God and will not repent
Slander and speaking evil — inner hostile speech against others or the gospel
Malice and wickedness as inner moral disposition — the wicked intent that must be put away
Slander as inner moral failure — speaking evil against others as an act of inner judgement and hostility
Doing evil as inner moral choice — the choice between good and harm as an inner-being decision
Term marks a plea addressed directly to God, giving voice to an inner state of need, urgency, fear, or penitence
Term marks an urgent entreaty between persons where the inner state of the speaker — fear, desperation, longing, moral distress — gives the request its force
Term names wickedness as a personal inner moral quality — the moral character of a person, whether denied, confessed, or attributed
Term names wickedness expressed outwardly in conduct — the inner moral quality manifested in acts or instruments of injustice
Term names the formation of purposeful intention in a human subject — what the mind resolves to do
Term names resolute divine purposing — intention formed and carried through without relenting
kol qualifies the totality of an inner moral condition — the complete inner life is characterised as wholly corrupt, proud, or godless, with no part exempt
kol qualifies the temporal or comprehensive persistence of an inner state — the state continues through all time or all circumstances, with no interruption or exception
kol universalises a grief, fear, terror, or relief response — the inner-being reaction to divine action or judgement is total, encompassing all persons without exception
kol qualifies the comprehensive scope of divine judgement against pride — every manifestation of inner pride is included, none exempt
kol qualifies the totality of inner-being response to God's word or presence — the universal inner act of attentiveness, worship, grief, or understanding before God
kol qualifies the comprehensive scope within which inner moral integrity is tested or maintained — through all that happened, the inner disposition held or was measured
kol qualifies the scope of a prayer, confession, or divine memory — the totality of deeds, iniquities, or acts is brought before God or held by God without remainder
Term voices inner awe or wonder — the question expresses the speaker's orientation of astonishment before what exceeds expectation or comprehension
Term voices inner terror or moral inadequacy before divine holiness or wrath — the question discloses the inner experience of being unable to stand before God
Term voices misplaced inner trust or fear — the question exposes or challenges a wrong inner orientation, tracing it to its consequences
Term voices the impossibility of inner self-knowledge — the heart's moral corruption exceeds human capacity to understand
Term voices inner helplessness before divine sovereignty and the inner recognition that God's will, word, or moral order cannot be turned back or challenged
Term voices the inner acts of grief, pity, and comfort — their presence or their devastating absence as a relational inner-being condition
Term voices the inner act of hearing, believing, or attending to the word of God — the inner-being conditions of spiritual receptivity and faith
Term voices inner contempt, defiance, or mockery — the inner orientation of rebellion against God or authority expressed through the rhetorical question
Term voices the inner moral standing and courage required to approach, challenge, or act before God or adversary
Term in optative use gives direct voice to inner longing, yearning, or lament — the form of the wish is the expression of the inner state
Escalating inner evil — the worse final state when evil returns to an unguarded person
Unrighteousness as inner moral condition — the state of suppressing truth and yielding to wrongdoing
Acting wrongly and inner-motivated misuse — asking wrongly, speaking evil, suffering the consequences of wickedness
Patient endurance of evil — the inner disposition of forbearance without resentment
Wrongdoing and suffering wrong — the inner moral act and its bearing on the one wronged and the wrongdoer

Term names the hypocrite as a person whose inner life is defined by disconnection between outward performance and inward reality — performing religious acts to be seen rather than from inner transformation
Term names the hypocrite as one who reads external signs but fails to discern the inner realities of God's action in history
Term names genuineness as an inner quality — the absence of outward performance disconnected from inner reality; love, faith, and wisdom being truly what they appear
Term names the act of joining in hypocrisy — the communal spread of a pretence that disconnects outward behaviour from inner reality
Term names the act of pretending — the deliberate inner intention to perform a quality one does not possess, with a hidden contrary purpose
Term names hypocrisy as an inner condition — the state of inner pretence in which outward performance is disconnected from actual inner reality, particularly in relation to God and others
Term names injustice as a moral condition — the pattern of wrong done by or to persons, which issues from the inner self and has inner consequences
Term names the unjust person — one whose inner character is oriented away from God and toward wickedness, with corresponding consequences
Term names acting unjustly — the inner orientation that produces perverse conduct, unable to perceive righteousness or divine majesty
Term names the unjust person — one whose inner character is defined by moral wickedness, into whose hands the innocent may be cast
Term names iniquity as a continuing inner pattern — the persistent orientation toward wrong that has characterised Israel since its early history
Term names injustice as an inner moral condition of the person — the twisting away from what is right, producing harm to others and alienation from God
Term names the absence of injustice in God — divine righteousness as the standard against which human injustice is measured
Term names the inner twisting or perversion — the bowing down of the person under suffering, or the moral warping of the way away from God
Term marks the inner departure or estrangement of the heart or people from God or the right moral way
Term marks the speaker's active distancing of self from wickedness or deliberate retention of moral integrity
Term marks whether inner or spiritual acts originate from God or self, and what is hidden or disclosed before God
Term marks the cause, extent, or privation of an inner state of suffering or its expressive manifestation
Term names the afflicted/poor person — one whose inner experience is defined by material need, oppression, and dependence on God for vindication and provision
Term names the afflicted person as the object of God's compassion — the one to whom God attends, comforts, and promises restoration
Term names perversity as an inner character orientation — the fundamental distortion of the person's heart, will, and speech toward evil and faithlessness
Term names perversity of speech as the outward expression of inner corruption — the perverted words that flow from and reveal a perverted inner life
Term names the crookedness or perverseness that destroys — the inner moral distortion that fractures both the self and others
Term names the devious inner character — the crooked orientation of the person whose ways depart from righteousness, expressing contempt for God or wisdom
Term names the act of turning away from wisdom — the inner movement of deviating from sound judgement and heart-orientation
Term names perversity of speech as an expression of inner moral deviation — the crooked orientation from which crooked talk flows
Term names the transformation of the inner person — the heart, mind, or character overturned, renewed, or corrupted
Term names the transformation of inner experience — mourning turned to joy, or joy to mourning, as God acts
Term names God's inner orientation overturn — God's heart recoiling, his compassion stirred, or his disposition changing toward or against his people
Term names the moral perversion of justice or speech — the overturn of right into wrong as an inner-motivated moral act
Term names a divine overthrow that should have produced inner return — the overwhelming judgment designed to evoke repentance, but which failed to move the heart
Term names a perverse contrariness — the inner orientation that inverts right relationship, giving what should not be given and withholding what should be received
Term names the perversity of the creature asserting itself against its Creator — the inner inversion of the proper creaturely orientation
Term names the crooked inner orientation of the guilty — a moral character quality expressed in the way one walks
Term names the perversion of moral discernment — the subverting of inner judgement and the cause of the righteous through corrupt influence
Term names the ruin brought by sin or folly upon the inner life — the self-destruction of the wicked through their own perversion
Term names sin as a moral-inner condition or state — the condition of wrongdoing that stands before God and requires forgiveness
Term names the act of causing another to stumble morally — the inner-relational damage done when one person's conduct triggers moral failure in another
Term names the act of sinning as a moral-volitional act — committing transgression against God, others, or the moral law, originating in the inner will
Term names a stumbling block — something that triggers inner-moral failure, hinders spiritual integrity, or constitutes an offense to faith
Negating fear, dread, and dismay — divine oracles and human petitions that qualify the inner state of fear as the term's direct object
Negating anger and displeasure — petition or command that the inner state of anger be withheld or restrained, directed at God or another person
Negating grief, mourning, sorrow, and weeping — inner states of lamentation directed against participation in or expression of sorrow
Negating rejoicing and exultation — prophetic prohibitions against inner joy that is misplaced, premature, or directed toward the wrong object
Negating trust, belief, and confidence — inner epistemic and relational orientation toward a person, word, or object
Negating pride, boasting, contempt, and scoffing — inner attitudes of self-elevation or dismissal
Negating concern, longing, and inner perceptual attitude — anxiety about goods, desire for harm, or minimisation of suffering
Negating cognitive and moral orientation — remembering, perceiving, understanding, moral turning — inner acts of mind and will
Negating inner-oriented expression — self-disclosing speech, prayer, and inner attitude expressed outward; including spirit-guarding and faithlessness
Term names sin as a concrete act or moral entity — a specific transgression that stands before God and carries moral weight
Term names wickedness as an inner-moral condition — the disposition of the person or community toward evil, which incurs divine judgment
Term names moral-spiritual completion or termination — the state of being blameless/complete in integrity, or the eschatological finishing of transgression and sin
Term names the inner-moral condition of sinlessness — the state of being without transgression, functioning as a criterion of inner-moral integrity

Transgression as the moral-theological category of human failing — the breaking of the law as a condition of inner moral culpability, including Adam's originating transgression and the transgressions from which Christ redeems
Without — negating inner states, moral conditions, and cognitive dispositions; including the absence of hatred in unintentional killing, the absence of fear, and being without transgression
Transgression noted but not punished — the inner moral condition of transgression as the object of divine discernment without immediate retribution

Trespass as the inner condition of moral death and the ground of reconciliation — the state of being dead in trespasses, and the grace-act of God not counting trespasses; including the single originating trespass of Adam and the many trespasses forgiven
Prophetic madness as the inner expression of lawlessness — the transgression of Balaam expressed as inner madness restrained by a donkey
The transgressor as a moral identity — being convicted, named, or revealed as one whose inner standing before the law is transgressive
Transgression as inner defection and deliberate moral override — the act of breaking a commandment or abiding teaching, including going beyond the teaching of Christ as an inner departure
Opening the lips as expression of inner recklessness and the opening of the self to moral corruption
The wicked's inner character — thoughts, heart, soul, desires directed toward evil
The way of the wicked — inner-being orientation expressed in life direction; the call to forsake it
Transgression speaking to the wicked deep in his heart — the internal voice of wickedness
The wicked in contrast to the righteous — moral antithesis in Psalms and Proverbs
"If the wicked turns from his way…he shall live" — Ezekiel's theology of moral turning
Divine incomparability expressed through comparison — "who is like you/me?" names God's unique inner-being character
"As yourself" — the reflexive inner-being standard for love of neighbour
Inner-being states compared — the heart hard as stone, desire like a heated oven, suffering like birth pangs
Job's inner-being comparisons — anguish, asymmetry, self-presentation, and character before God
Acting wickedly — inner act of moral departure from God and righteousness
Condemnation and guilt — the verdict of wickedness on the inner moral condition
Lawlessness as an inner disposition requiring forgiveness and forming a bond of slavery
Loving righteousness and hating wickedness — inner dispositions in contrast
Wickedness as defining inner character
Term characterises a person by the inner disposition and act of betrayal — treachery as a defining moral quality of the inner person
